title: Hello World Express scaffold
what_built: A minimal Express server scaffold with one route returning "Hello world" and a static file directory for /public. Just the foundation — no auth, no game, no database. Just proof that the project structure works and Replit can run it.
what_was_hard:
The hardest part was unwinding what Replit's "create a new project" template gave me. The starter was a TypeScript pnpm monorepo with Vite and a bunch of scaffolding I didn't need. I wiped it and rebuilt as plain Node + Express to match the PRD's "vanilla JavaScript only" constraint.

The .replit file had stale [[ports]] entries pointing at Vite's dev server port (5173) and the workflow command was set to pnpm-related scripts. I had to manually edit .replit to map localPort=5000 (where my Express server binds) to externalPort=80 (what Replit's preview proxy expects). Without that the preview just showed "Page not found." Took some Googling to figure out the right format.
what_id_do_differently: Read the PRD's "allowed packages" list FIRST before clicking around in Replit. I wasted an hour fighting the TypeScript template before I realized the right move was to delete it and start fresh.
ai_usage: Did the cleanup mostly by hand at first because I didn't yet have Claude Code installed locally. Used Replit's built-in AI to help understand the .replit port mapping syntax. Once Claude Code was set up on my MacBook, the workflow stabilized for CP02 onwards.
ab_note: First instinct was to keep the TypeScript starter because it looked "more professional." Decided against it because the PRD is explicit: vanilla JavaScript only. Ignoring that constraint to feel fancier would have caused problems by CP03 when Claude Code started writing JS that the TypeScript build would have rejected. Sticking close to the PRD's constraints early saved pain later.

title: Signup, login, logout with sessions
what_built: Signup, login, and logout with express-session middleware. Users are persisted as plaintext records in data/users.json (per the PRD's learning-project rule). The frontend has signup/login forms that swap out for a logout button when the session is active, and refreshes its status by hitting GET /me on page load.
what_was_hard: First time using Claude Code locally — had to install Node.js, npm, Homebrew, GitHub CLI, and authenticate everything before I could even start. Hit an EACCES npm permissions issue from running an earlier sudo command that I had to fix with chown.
what_id_do_differently: Read PRs more carefully on the first pass. I almost merged with req.session.user being a string instead of an object — it would have worked for CP02 but broken later when CP05 tries to attach games to a user.
ai_usage: Claude Code wrote the implementation on a cp02-accounts branch. I reviewed the diff against the PRD, caught the req.session.user shape inconsistency, and asked Claude Code to fix it before merging. Did not blindly approve the first PR.
ab_note: First version of the prompt I sent to Claude Code was vague — just "build CP02." Rewrote it with explicit constraints (allowed packages, plaintext passwords, exact route status codes, branch + PR workflow). The second prompt got a clean implementation on the first try.

title: 3x3 grid clickable game board
what_built: A 3x3 clickable board with a turn indicator and "New game" button. Game state lives as an array of 9 strings in memory, plus a currentTurn variable that flips between X and O. The board is hidden when logged out; logging in resets to a fresh empty board. Clicking an empty cell places the mark and flips the turn; clicking a marked cell does nothing. No win detection yet — that's CP04.
what_was_hard: Understanding why Claude Code used one click listener on #board instead of nine separate listeners on each cell. Had to look up event delegation and how .closest() walks up the DOM to find the actual clickable element.
what_id_do_differently: Probably write the click handler myself first to make sure I understood event delegation before using it. The pattern is clearly the right call, but it was new to me.
ai_usage: Claude Code wrote the implementation on a cp03-game branch. I reviewed the diff carefully — verified that no win-detection logic snuck in (that's CP04), confirmed the new-game button was inside #game so it'd hide on logout, and traced the disabled-state pattern from JS through CSS.
ab_note: Originally wanted a click-to-preview-then-confirm pattern. Talked through the tradeoffs — it would double click counts on a 9-click game and add complexity for the AI in CP06 — and switched to instant click with hover affordance. Better choice, simpler code.

title: Win and draw detection
what_built: Win and draw detection on top of CP03's clickable board. Defined WIN_LINES as the 8 winning index triples (3 rows, 3 columns, 2 diagonals). After every move, checkWinner loops through the lines looking for three matching marks. A gameOver flag freezes the whole board when someone wins or the board fills without a winner. The turn indicator text changes to "X wins!", "O wins!", or "Draw" depending on outcome.
what_was_hard: Making sure three empty cells in a row don't register as a win — the `board[a] && ...` check at the start of each line comparison handles that. Subtle bug I would have shipped if Claude Code hadn't gotten it right the first time.
what_id_do_differently: Look up the standard winning-lines pattern earlier — it's the same in basically every tic tac toe tutorial and would have saved me time thinking about it.
ai_usage: Claude Code wrote the implementation on a cp04-pvp branch. I reviewed the makeMove function carefully — checked that turn flip happens only when game is still in progress, not on the winning move. Also checked that the indicator text update was moved out of renderBoard into the state-changing functions, which is cleaner separation than my CP03 version had.
ab_note: First version of CP03 had the indicator text inside renderBoard. CP04 moved it out into makeMove and newGame so renderBoard is purely about syncing cells from state. Cleaner — one function per concern.

title: Persist games to JSON, show user history
what_built:
Server-side persistence for finished games. Added POST /games and GET /games routes to server.js. POST validates the request body (result must be "win" or "draw", board must be a 9-element array), requires login (401 if not), takes the username from the session and the timestamp from the server clock (intentionally not from the request body — that would let a logged-in user submit games as someone else or backdate wins). GET filters to only the current user's games and returns newest first.

On the frontend, added a saveGame() function that POSTs to /games when a game ends in win or draw, then immediately calls loadHistory() to refresh the visible list. Added a "Your game history" section that renders past games with formatted timestamps. History clears on logout and reloads on login.
what_was_hard:
After implementing CP05, games saved to data/games.json correctly but the history list on the page wouldn't update right after a win — only on a full page refresh. Browser console showed 401 Unauthorized errors on /games requests, even though /me worked fine and the user was clearly logged in. Spent significant time debugging this:

1. First attempted fix: added credentials: 'same-origin' to all fetch() calls. Helped some but games still saved intermittently and the immediate GET still failed sometimes.

2. Second attempted fix (PR #7): switched to credentials: 'include' + sameSite: 'none' + secure: true on the server cookie. This is the standard pattern for cross-origin iframe cookies. Broke login entirely because secure: true requires HTTPS in a way Replit's preview proxy doesn't fully support. Reverted with git revert -m 1.

3. Tested in incognito (outside the Replit iframe) to isolate code vs environment. The first game saved but subsequent games still didn't appear immediately, which suggested the issue might not just be the iframe.

4. Reviewed all my files line by line with help to verify the code itself was correct — POST validation, session handling, GET filtering, fetch options, etc. Everything checked out. Deduced the most likely root cause is that express-session stores sessions in memory by default, and Replit's free tier restarts the workflow process unpredictably, which wipes all in-memory sessions. When the server boots back up, my old session ID is in the cookie but the server has no record of it = 401.

5. Considered adding a file-based session store (session-file-store) but that would violate the PRD's allowed-packages rule. Decided to stop debugging on my own and ask my teacher Monday — this seems to be a Replit-specific environment issue, not a code issue, and they may have experience with the right setup.

[Final state at end of session:]
- Signup, login, logout work correctly
- Games save correctly to data/games.json on win or draw
- Game history displays correctly when the page is loaded or refreshed
- History does NOT consistently auto-update right after a win (intermittent 401s on /games requests, suspected to be sessions being lost when Replit restarts the server process)
what_id_do_differently:
1. Open Chrome DevTools earlier in the debugging — I spent time guessing at what was broken before actually looking at Network tab status codes. Once I started reading the responses, the diagnosis got way faster.

2. Test in incognito earlier to separate code bugs from environment bugs. That single test would have saved me hours of trying cookie configs.

3. Recognize sooner when "one more fix" stops being productive. I should have stopped after PR #7 broke login and asked for outside help instead of assuming there was a code-level fix I was missing.

4. Always merge a PR immediately after it's approved, before doing anything else. At one point I had a PR open while testing in Replit, and when the next fix was needed Claude Code branched from a stale main, polluting the diff with already-merged code. Had to close that PR and redo.

5. Be more skeptical of intermittent bugs. When a bug happens "sometimes," that often means the code is correct and something environmental is flaky. Treating intermittent failures the same as deterministic bugs wasted time.
ai_usage:
Claude Code wrote all the implementation. Each PR was reviewed before merging — I caught a workflow mistake (PR branched from stale main → confusing diff → close and redo), and verified all the security-sensitive parts of the /games routes (auth gating, server-controlled username, server-controlled timestamp, user-scoped GET filter). The code that shipped is correct. The bugs we hit were not code bugs.

When the cookie issue first appeared, I asked Claude Code to fix it. It produced a clean credentials: 'same-origin' patch that helped partially. When that wasn't enough, it produced a full iframe-cookie fix that was technically correct but incompatible with Replit's preview environment.
ab_note:
- First diagnosis: "POST /games is broken." Wrong — POST works.
- Second diagnosis: "Browser cache problem." Wrong — caching wasn't the issue.
- Third diagnosis: "Cookies aren't being sent (same-origin)." Partially right — fixed some symptoms.
- Fourth diagnosis: "Need full cross-origin cookie config (include + sameSite: none + secure: true)." Technically correct but broke login in Replit.
- Final diagnosis: "Replit's environment restarts the server, wiping in-memory sessions. The fix is probably either a different session store or some Replit-specific setting." Asking teacher for help with this.

[Lesson:]
Not all bugs are code bugs. The most expensive part of this checkpoint wasn't writing the feature — it was figuring out that what looked like a code bug was actually an infrastructure quirk. Real engineers ship with known limitations and document them. That's also a valid outcome.

title: Groq-powered AI opponent
what_built: AI opponent powered by Groq's llama-3.3-70b-versatile model. Added a PvP/PvAI mode toggle to the UI. When in PvAI mode and it's the AI's turn, the browser POSTs the current board state to a new /ai-move route. The server builds a structured prompt for Groq (showing the board layout, the current state, and the explicit list of legal moves), forces JSON-only output via response_format, parses the move, and validates it's a legal empty cell. If Groq returns an invalid move or the API call fails, the server retries up to 3 times, then falls back to picking a random legal cell. This guarantees the PRD's "AI plays legal moves" requirement holds even if the LLM misbehaves.
what_was_hard:
The architecture decision around where to call Groq. If the Groq SDK ran in the browser, the API key would be exposed in client-side JavaScript — anyone could view source and steal it. The right pattern is to call Groq on the server, where the API key stays in .env. The browser sends only the board state, gets back only the chosen move. Standard server-side secret pattern.

Also had to think about UX during AI turns. Without locking the board, the user could click cells while the AI was "thinking", causing race conditions. Added an aiThinking boolean flag that gates user clicks, released in a try/finally so it always unlocks even on error.
what_id_do_differently: Probably show a "thinking..." indicator while the AI is computing its move. Right now there's just a 1-2 second pause where nothing visible happens, which could feel like a bug. Could be a small CP07 or CP09 polish item.
ai_usage:
Claude Code wrote the implementation cleanly in one PR. The /ai-move route had all the security checks I wanted (auth required, body validation, server-set move validation against available cells), the prompt engineering was solid (showed the board visually, listed legal moves explicitly, forced JSON output), and the retry-with-fallback logic guaranteed legal moves even on LLM failure.

I caught one architectural decision in the prompt that mattered: I specified the API call MUST happen server-side, not browser-side. Without that constraint, Claude Code could have built it either way and we'd have had an exposed API key.
ab_note: Considered letting the player pick X or O. Decided to keep CP06 simple (player always X, AI always O, X always first) because the rubric is "AI plays legal moves" and letter-choice doesn't help that. Saving "player picks letter" as a possible CP09 custom feature where it would actually count toward the 30% custom-features rubric.

title: 3 difficulties + 3 personalities, hybrid AI
what_built:
Three difficulty levels (easy/medium/hard) and three AI personalities (pirate/wizard/robot), independently selectable via dropdowns visible only in vs AI mode. The AI now returns BOTH a move AND a short comment in character. The comment displays below the board after each AI move and clears when the player moves or starts a new game.

[Architecture:]
Difficulty controls how the AI PLAYS (skill level via prompt instructions + temperature tuning: easy=1.2, medium=0.7, hard=0.3). Personality controls how the AI TALKS (commentary tone via vocabulary instructions). They are completely independent — easy pirate, hard robot, medium wizard, all 9 combinations work.

The prompt is built modularly — base instructions about the board layout, plus a difficulty-specific paragraph, plus a personality-specific paragraph, plus a structured JSON output requirement. This means adding a 4th personality later is just a new entry in two constants, not a rewrite.
what_was_hard:
The biggest design question was where to put the difficulty/personality logic. Three options I considered:
1. Hardcoded difficulty (just always medium) and pick personality randomly — too simple
2. Difficulty as separate algorithm paths (easy = random, hard = minimax) — would work but bypasses Groq for half the modes, which felt like missing the point of having an LLM opponent
3. Difficulty as prompt engineering — let Groq adjust based on instructions + temperature

Went with option 3. It keeps Groq as the decision-maker for all modes, which means the experience feels consistent and the personalities can sass you about every move. Temperature tuning (1.2 vs 0.3) reinforces the difficulty in a way the prompt alone can't — high temperature makes easy mode's "sometimes pick random" actually random, while low temperature makes hard mode's "always block" actually consistent.

[Robust fallbacks:]
If Groq returns invalid JSON, missing move, missing comment, or fails entirely, the server falls back per-personality. A pirate AI never suddenly says "MOVE EXECUTED" — even on total failure, you get "Arrr, that be me move." Took some defensive coding to validate move AND comment independently.

[Update — hard mode fix:]
After tagging CP07-levels initially, I tested and discovered hard mode wasn't actually playing well. The LLM consistently failed to block obvious threats — even with explicit "always block two-in-a-row" instructions in the prompt. I'd play X in cell 0, then X in cell 1, and the AI wouldn't play cell 2 to block. This made hard mode no harder than easy mode in practice.

Diagnosed it as a fundamental LLM limitation: language models don't have reliable spatial reasoning. Reading a JSON-encoded board state and mentally mapping positions 0-8 to a 3x3 grid is something LLMs do inconsistently. Even GPT-4 and Claude make this mistake regularly.

Fixed it with a hybrid architecture: deterministic game logic for tactical situations (JS function findTacticalMove that checks all 8 winning lines for "two-in-a-row with one cell empty"), LLM for everything else. Hard mode now: (1) takes immediate wins, (2) blocks opponent threats, (3) only asks Groq for a strategic move when there's no immediate tactical situation. Personality commentary still comes from Groq — even on forced JS moves — so the AI still sounds like a pirate/wizard/robot.

Big lesson: LLMs are great at language and pattern-matching. They're bad at deterministic logic. Use the right tool for each job. This is actually how real game AI gets built — neural nets for "intuition" combined with hard-coded rules for situations where the right answer is provable.

Easy and medium modes were left fully Groq-driven on purpose. The LLM's imperfection IS the difficulty curve. Trying to force the LLM to play worse on easy mode would have been weird; just letting it occasionally miss things naturally produces "easy" behavior.

Re-tagged CP07-levels at the post-fix commit so the tag points at the working version.
what_id_do_differently:
The comment displays as static text below the board. A subtle fade-in animation would make it feel more alive. Saving for CP10 polish.

Also, currently the player has to wait for the comment to appear THEN the AI's mark animates in. The order is: AI calculates → comment displays → mark appears. Could be smoother. Could also display the comment as a speech bubble next to the board instead of below it.
ai_usage: Wrote a really long prompt for claude.ai/code that spelled out all 9 combinations of difficulty + personality, the validation requirements, the fallback comments, the dropdown UI, and the comment display logic. It got everything right in one PR. Worth noting that I tested this checkpoint on the school iMac using claude.ai/code in browser instead of Claude Code in terminal — same agent, different interface, identical output quality.
ab_note: Considered fancier difficulty implementations (separate code paths for each level, minimax algorithm for hard mode). Decided prompt + temperature was cleaner because: (a) it kept Groq as the decision-maker for consistency, (b) made the implementation 30 lines of constants instead of 200 lines of game tree code, and (c) the LLM playing imperfectly is actually MORE fun than perfect minimax which always draws.

title: Leaderboard, AI stats, Checkpoints page
what_built:
Three features in one checkpoint: a public leaderboard ranking players by win rate (5-game minimum), AI win rate breakdowns by difficulty and personality, and a Checkpoints page that renders all my reflections from a structured JSON file (data/reflections.json). Also augmented game records to track mode/difficulty/personality so the AI stats have data to aggregate. New routes (/leaderboard, /ai-stats, /reflections) are public so the leaderboard works without login — fits how leaderboards typically work in classroom settings.

[A note on scope:]
The PRD says CP08 is "leaderboard + AI win rate by difficulty/personality." I bundled the Checkpoints page into the same PR even though it's not explicitly part of CP08's spec, because the PRD requires a Checkpoints page as a deliverable but doesn't assign it to a specific checkpoint. Bundling it here ensured the page existed before any more reflections were written. The alternative was making it a CP09 custom feature, but I had stronger ideas for CP09 and CP10 that involve actual gameplay features (player picks X/O for CP09, styling pass + game replay for CP10).
what_was_hard:
The data structure decision. Reflections could have been a single markdown file rendered with a library, or hardcoded HTML, or a structured JSON with named fields. I went with structured JSON because: (1) it matches how the rest of the app stores data (games.json, users.json), (2) editing reflections later means just editing a JSON file rather than touching HTML, (3) the rendering code is small and reusable across all reflections, (4) adding a new field updates how all reflections display.

The leaderboard logic for PvP win attribution is imperfect — games.json records "username" (the saver) and "winner" (X or O) but doesn't track which user was which letter in PvP. For CP08 I treat the saver as X since the AI is always O in our system, which means PvP wins are correctly attributed to the saver if they were X. PvP games where the saver was O get incorrectly counted. Documented this as a known limitation; would fix with a proper "playerLetter" field on game records in a future iteration.

Old games from earlier testing don't have mode/difficulty/personality fields. AI stats route gracefully skips them via the `if (game.mode !== 'pvai' || !game.difficulty || !game.personality) continue;` check. Old games still count toward the leaderboard's player win counts.
what_id_do_differently: Built the Checkpoints page from CP01 onward instead of waiting until CP08. The PRD called it out as a deliverable but didn't assign it a checkpoint, so I missed it for the first 7 checkpoints. Catching it at CP08 was salvageable but I should have flagged it earlier.
ai_usage: Wrote a 200+ line prompt for Claude Code with three features specified separately, hard constraints, the data model changes, the public/private route distinction, edge cases for old game data, and the reflection file structure. Got a clean PR back in one shot — 9 file changes, all working as specified, no constraint violations. Reviewed the diff carefully; only minor optional polish items I chose not to fix.
ab_note: Considered hard-coding reflections in HTML before settling on JSON storage. Chat reviewer (the second AI in my AI-checks-AI workflow) pushed back hard on the HTML approach for maintainability reasons — editing HTML for content changes is the exact thing engineers spend careers learning to avoid. Sticking with JSON was clearly correct in hindsight; same pattern as games.json in our app already.

title: Player picks X or O (custom feature 1)
what_built:
A letter selector that lets the player choose to be X or O before each game. The AI plays the other letter. If player picks O, AI plays X and goes first — AI moves automatically when New Game is clicked, no extra interaction needed. The CP07 personalities and difficulty levels work the same way regardless of which letter the AI is playing.

This also retroactively fixed a known limitation in CP08's leaderboard: game records previously didn't track which letter the player chose, so PvP win attribution was guessed (assuming saver was always X). Now playerLetter is saved on every game record and the leaderboard route uses it for accurate attribution. Old game records (from before this change) fall back to assuming X — backwards compatible.
what_was_hard:
The trickiest part was getting the trigger conditions right. The AI's turn fires based on currentTurn matching aiLetter, not based on a hardcoded 'O'. The "clear AI comment" check fires when player moves, not when X moves. The "AI moves first" trigger fires from newGame() when AI is X, not from the cell click handler. Each of these had been hardcoded to 'X' or 'O' in CP07, and missing any of them would have broken the feature subtly (AI never moves, AI moves at wrong time, comments clear at wrong time).

The Groq prompt also needed parameterization. The original CP07 prompt said "You are playing Tic Tac Toe as player O" — hardcoded. With CP09 this becomes dynamic based on aiLetter. Same for the hard-mode tactical override in findTacticalMove — passes aiLetter as the parameter instead of hardcoded 'O'.
what_id_do_differently:
The mini-PRD specified that the letter dropdown is "visible always." For CP09 that's a flat dropdown next to the mode toggle. It works but feels visually busy alongside the difficulty/personality dropdowns when in PvAI mode. Will redesign in CP10's styling pass — likely combine all settings (mode, letter, difficulty, personality) into a single options panel or modal.

Could have also added stats tracking by letter ("X-as-player win rate vs O-as-player win rate") to make the choice feel more measurable. Decided that was scope creep for CP09; deferred as a possible future feature.
ai_usage: Wrote a 200+ line prompt for Claude Code with the architectural overview, hard constraints, the data flow changes (frontend + backend), the prompt parameterization, the leaderboard backwards-compat handling, and the mini-PRD content. Got a clean PR back in one shot — 5 file changes including the mini-PRD doc, all working as specified.
ab_note:
Initial plan was a separate "setup screen" with a Start button before each game (Q&A in chat preferred this). Chat reviewer pushed back on the setup-screen approach — too much state machinery for what is just "player can pick a letter," and it would have conflicted with how the rest of the UI works. Settled on the simpler approach (visible dropdown, default X, change resets game). The setup-screen-style redesign is now planned as part of CP10's styling pass where it makes more sense.

[Mini-PRD as a deliverable:]
Documented the feature in cp09-prd.md including out-of-scope items. The "what's NOT in this feature" list turned out to be useful for staying focused — it explicitly defers animation, mid-game letter change, stats by letter, and game replay, all of which are tempting scope creep.

title: Dark redesign + game replay (custom feature 2)
what_built:
Two related deliverables in one final checkpoint: (1) a complete visual redesign across all three pages (dark theme, electric green accent, unified settings panel, motion polish), and (2) a game replay feature that lets the player click any past game in their history and watch the moves play out move-by-move with prev/next/auto-play controls.

The redesign isn't just a re-skin — it consolidated the four scattered selectors from CP07 and CP09 (mode toggle, letter selector, difficulty dropdown, personality dropdown) into a single labeled "Game Settings" panel. CSS classes show/hide difficulty and personality based on mode, so the panel looks clean in both PvP and PvAI. Three motion details (AI mark fade-in, AI-thinking dots indicator, winning line green glow) make the app feel responsive instead of static.

The replay feature uses existing data — no schema changes, no new server routes. Game records save the final board, so the replay reconstructs the move sequence by interleaving X cells and O cells in left-to-right top-to-bottom order. This is positional order, not click order — the final board state matches what actually happened, but the path to get there is approximated. Documented honestly as a known limitation in cp10-prd.md.
what_was_hard:
Two technically interesting problems.

First, the replay button event listeners. Each time openReplay() is called, it needs to wire up Prev/Next/Auto-play to the current game's data. If we just .addEventListener every time, listeners stack up across replay opens — opening the modal three times means each Next click advances three steps. Solution: clone each button via cloneNode, replace the original in the DOM, then attach the listener to the clone. This guarantees a clean slate every open. Took me a minute to realize this was happening — symptoms of stale closures often look like "the feature works the first time then gets weirder."

Second, the AI mark fade-in animation. Removing then immediately re-adding a CSS class doesn't restart the animation — the browser batches DOM changes and sees the class state as unchanged. The fix is forcing a reflow with `void cell.offsetWidth` between the remove and add. This is a documented browser quirk — the offsetWidth read forces the layout engine to commit pending changes, which "counts" the class removal so the re-add triggers a fresh animation.

The whole replay UI also taught me about modal accessibility. Backdrop clicks need to distinguish "click on backdrop" from "click bubbled up from content" — solved by checking event.target === modal element. Autoplay intervals must be cleared on close OR they keep running and mutate state in the closed modal. Buttons need to disable at boundaries to prevent currentMoveIndex from going negative.
what_id_do_differently:
Save move sequence on game records going forward (a `moveOrder` array saved when the game ends). The replay reconstruction is fine for old games but doesn't show true click order. With moveOrder saved, buildReplayMoves() could just return moveOrder directly for new games and fall back to positional reconstruction for old. About 30 minutes of work that I deliberately deferred.

Could have done a light mode toggle. Decided dark-only was a stronger design statement and easier to keep consistent. Two themes means double the styling burden and twice the chance of broken contrast.

The mobile responsiveness is "doesn't visibly break" but isn't specifically tuned. The 720px max-width and the 2-column settings grid both adapt reasonably to narrow screens but I didn't add explicit breakpoints.

[Update — true replay follow-up:]
After CP10 shipped, I tested the replay feature and noticed that games where I clicked in non-positional order (center first, then corners) replayed in left-to-right top-to-bottom order instead of my actual click sequence. The replay LOOKED right but didn't show what actually happened.

Followed up with a small fix: capture moveOrder as the game is played (every makeMove call pushes to a moveOrder array), save it alongside the final board, and use it in replay when present. Old games (saved before this fix) gracefully fall back to the positional reconstruction in buildReplayMoves.

Server-side validation was important here — moveOrder is structured data the client could send badly, so each entry gets strict type checks (index 0-8, mark X or O), plus a length sanity check that moveOrder count must match the marks on the final board. Wrong client data → 400, never silently saved.

Re-tagged CP10-c2 at the post-fix commit so the canonical CP10 points at the version with true replay.

Lesson: "shipped with documented limitation" doesn't always mean "don't fix the limitation." If the fix is small and addresses the actual user experience, ship the fix and update the documentation. The mini-PRD's "out of scope" list isn't permanent — it's a snapshot of what the FIRST version included.
ai_usage:
Wrote the largest prompt of the project — 300+ lines specifying the design tokens, every color, the settings panel structure, the replay state machine, the animations, the regression tests, and the mini-PRD. Got back a clean PR with all features working in one shot. The fact that it landed cleanly says something about the value of detailed specs: I was essentially handing Claude Code a design document, not a vague request.

The chat reviewer (in my AI-checks-AI workflow) caught the move-reconstruction limitation during diff review and suggested I either ship it as-is, augment with moveOrder, or remove auto-play. Decided ship-as-is + document was the right call for a project at the finish line.
ab_note:
Original CP10 plan was a separate setup screen for picking letter (deferred from CP09) — chat reviewer pushed back on that as overengineering. Settled on the unified settings panel which solves the same UX problem (all options grouped) without the state machine complexity of a separate screen. Cleaner architecture, easier to implement, looks more polished.

Considered themed coloring per AI personality (pirate = red, wizard = purple, robot = cyan). Decided single accent (#4ade80) was better — visual coherence across personalities, no color palette explosion, easier to review the diff. Personality is communicated through text (commentary) which is the right channel.

Considered light mode. Decided dark-only was a stronger design statement and easier to keep consistent.
